:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends

This section provides a comprehensive look at the dynamics of Missouri's prison admissions, releases, and population, highlighting key aspects such as admission types, demographic breakdowns, criminal offenses, and sentence lengths.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Number of Admissions ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.


``` ::: ::: ### Number of Releases ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about change in releases from 2010 to 2020.


``` ::: ::: ### Year-End Population ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about change in year-end population from 2010 to 2020.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Missouri in 2020, people in prison for new court commitments accounted for 75% of the prison population, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 25%.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Demographics

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethinicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offense

Sentence about most common offense.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Length

Sentence about most common sentence length.



``` ::: :::

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Offenders convicted of class C or D felonies related to drugs, DWI, or other non- violent crimes are generally eligible for release after 15% of their sentence has been served. Offenders convicted of class A or B non-violent or drug felonies, or class B DWIs are eligible for release after 25% of their sentence has been served. If a parolee is serving consecutive sentences, the minimum term for eligibility for parole must be calculated by adding the minimum terms for each individual sentence together; however, this must not exceed the minimum term for parole eligibility for an ordinary life sentence. Several types of convictions make offenders ineligible for parole; these include convictions for first-degree murder, tampering with a victim or witness, certain recidivist drug trafficking offenses, and conviction as a persis- tent sexual offender. Circuit courts have jurisdiction to grant parole to offenders serving sentences under 2 1/2 years (in other words, those serving non-prison sentences). There do not appear to be guidelines or other criteria for court-based parole release.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years
3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Focusing on New Court Commitments with
Sentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 2,796 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 22% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Demographics

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, White, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 62 percent.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 91 percent.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 36 percent.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 5 to 9.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 44 percent.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with rape or sexual assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 29 percent.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.
``` ::: :::
